where-ocean-plastic-comes-from: Most plastic in the sea starts on land.
Trash washes down drains and rivers into the ocean, where it breaks into tiny bits called microplastics that stick around for a very long time.
impact-on-marine-animals: A floating plastic bag looks like a tasty jellyfish to a turtle, and birds think small bits are food.
Eating plastic makes animals sick, and old fishing nets can trap whales and seals.
solutions-that-work: The best thing to do is use less plastic in the first place.
Reusing things, cleaning up beaches, and rules like bag bans all help too.
reading-the-red-list: Scientists sort animals by how much danger they're in - from 'doing fine' all the way to 'almost gone.' It's based on how many are left and whether their numbers are dropping.
why-species-decline: Animals get in trouble mostly because of people: losing their homes, being hunted too much, pollution, and a changing climate.
The good news is that people can fix these things too.
recovery-is-possible: Pandas, tigers, and humpback whales are all doing better now because people protected their homes and stopped the hunting.
reading-labels: Special labels help you pick kinder products - like ones that don't hurt forests or animals.
Labels aren't perfect, but they push companies to do better.
high-impact-swaps: Some choices help way more than others.
Eating less beef and picking good seafood matter a lot more than tiny swaps.
Put your effort where it counts most.
avoiding-greenwashing: Words like 'eco' and 'natural' don't really mean much on their own.
Look for clear proof, and remember: buying less is often better than buying a 'greener' version of more.
what-biodiversity-is: Biodiversity means lots of different kinds of life.
The more variety there is, the stronger nature is at handling problems like disease or drought.
why-it-matters-to-people: Nature gives us food, medicine, clean water, and helps flowers grow.
These gifts are worth a fortune and are really hard to replace.
protecting-it-locally: You can help right where you live - plant native flowers, skip the bug spray, keep cats inside, and support green spaces near you.
marine-protected-areas: Some parts of the ocean are set aside as safe zones where fishing is limited.
Fish there grow bigger and have more babies, and some swim out to fill nearby waters too.
sustainable-fishing: Catching too many fish is the ocean's biggest problem.
Smarter nets and catch limits let us still get food without harming as much wildlife.
the-30x30-goal: Countries promised to protect 3 out of every 10 parts of the sea and land by the year 2030.
We're getting there, but not fast enough - so speaking up helps.
what-drives-the-trade: Some people illegally buy and sell animal parts like ivory and horns, or wild animals as pets.
This is a huge problem that can push animals toward disappearing forever.
how-it-s-fought: Rangers, sniffer dogs, and special laws help stop this trade.
But the real fix is getting people to stop wanting to buy these things.
what-you-can-do: Never buy things made from wild animals.
If shoppers stop buying, the hunters stop hunting.
why-cities-matter-for-wildlife: Gardens, parks, and green roofs join up to make homes for foxes, bats, birds, and bees.
Lots of small friendly spaces linked together work best.
coexisting-without-conflict: Most trouble with wild animals is about food and gaps.
Keep bins closed, don't leave pet food out, and block off holes kindly before animals move in.
Never use poison - it travels up and kills the owls and foxes that eat pests for free.
helping-your-wild-neighbours: Little things add up: a gap in the fence for hedgehogs, a bug-spray-free patch, a dish of water, native flowers, and stickers so birds don't hit windows.
the-ride-you-shouldn-t-take: Elephants that carry people were treated very badly as babies to make them obey.
Real elephant homes never offer rides or shows - you just watch elephants being elephants, which is even better.
selfies-and-sanctuaries: Places that let you hug tigers or hold wild-animal babies are usually hurting them behind the scenes.
Real animal homes don't let visitors touch - the animals get to just live their lives.
souvenirs-that-cost-lives: Some souvenirs are made from animal parts, like elephant tusks or sea-turtle shells.
Buying them hurts animals and can even get you in trouble at the airport.
Choose local crafts or sweets instead!
how-to-travel-wild-kindly: Pick tours that watch animals from far away, never feed wild animals, use sunscreen that's safe for coral, and choose local guides.
Your trip becomes a gift to nature.
